coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal synthesis of bifunctional carbon dots for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection from chamomile residue by ternary DES pretreatment
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zheng
Zheng, Han-Yi
Huang, Zhong-Ying
Liu, Yue-Zhen
Liu, Yao-Hua
Chen, Yun
Gao, Jing
Hu, Yong
Huang, Chao
coredata.dc:description: A ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES) consisting of choline chloride, lactic acid, and urea in a molar ratio of 1:2:2 was used to pretreat chamomile residue, followed by carbon dots (CDs) preparation using a one-pot solvothermal method. The CDs prepared under the suitable conditions had a high quantum yield of 47.34% and could be used as a bifunctional fluorescent probe for the detection of tartrazine and Fe(III). The concentration of tartrazine or Fe(III) had a good linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity of CDs that the determination coefficient (R 2) was 0.9957 and 0.9943, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 40 nM and 119 nM, respectively. After verifying the different fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs by these two substances, a quantitative analysis was performed on real samples with recoveries of 90.70%∼104.29%. Overall, this study provided a promising technology for chemical conversion from low-cost chamomile residue to attractive bifunctional fluorescent probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deep eutectic solvent
Fluorescent probe
Tartrazine
Fe(III)
Fluorescence quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The influence of DES pretreatment on the synthesis of CDs
Structure characterization of chamomile residue derived CDs
Optical properties of chamomile residue derived CDs
Fluorescence quenching of CDs by tartrazine and Fe(III)
Detection of tartrazine and Fe(III) in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by Special project in key fields of colleges and universities in Guangdong Province (2021ZDZX4001), the Science and Technology Program of Zhongshan (2021B2001, 2020B2068, 2021B2048), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019A1515011843, 2019A1515010640, 2021A1515110999), Special Support Project of Guangdong Province (2016TQ03N881), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078070).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of CDs; (B) particle size distribution of CDs; (C) XRD spectrum of CDs; (D) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (E) XPS spectrum of CDs; (F) high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s; (G) high resolution XPS spectrum of O1s; (H) high resolution XPS spectrum of N1s.

(A) Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. Inset: image of CDs solution without and with UV irradiation (365 nm); (B) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs with various excitation wavelength from 330 to 400 nm; (C) fluorescence intensity of CDs in 15 days; (D) fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH values; (E) fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentrations of Na+; (F) fluorescence intensity of CDs at different temperatures.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of CDs after addition of different food additives; (B) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs after addition of different azo colorants; (C) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations of tartrazine; (D) the linear correlation between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentration of tartrazine; (E) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs after addition of different metal ions; (F) the linear correlation between F0/F and the concentration of Fe(III); (G) F0/F of five parallel samples for detecting tartrazine or Fe(III).

(A) Fluorescence lifetime decay of CDs with and without tartrazine or sunset yellow; (B) fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of CDs with different azo colorants at various temperatures (5 °C, room temperature, 70 °C); (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs with different azo colorants; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Fe(III) and CDs + Fe(III) system; (E) zeta potentials of CDs with and without Fe(III). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Effect of DES composition, pretreatment condition, and reaction condition on the QY of CDs.
Results of detection by CDs for actual sample assays (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Sum of the spiked amount and the initial concentration of Fe(III) in the sample. The initial concentration of Fe(III) in the lake and the piped water measured by ICP-MS was 0.223 μM and 0.106 μM, respectively.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal synthesis of bifunctional carbon dots for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection from chamomile residue by ternary DES pretreatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan 528458, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan 528458, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: GDPU-HKU Zhongshan Biomedical Innovation Platform, Zhongshan 528437, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: GDPU-HKU Zhongshan Biomedical Innovation Platform, Zhongshan 528437, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: No. 9 Changmingshui Avenue, Wuguishan Street, Zhongshan 528458, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES) consisting of choline chloride, lactic acid, and urea in a molar ratio of 1:2:2 was used to pretreat chamomile residue, followed by carbon dots (CDs) preparation using a one-pot solvothermal method. The CDs prepared under the suitable conditions had a high quantum yield of 47.34% and could be used as a bifunctional fluorescent probe for the detection of tartrazine and Fe(III). The concentration of tartrazine or Fe(III) had a good linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity of CDs that the determination coefficient (R 2) was 0.9957 and 0.9943, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 40 nM and 119 nM, respectively. After verifying the different fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs by these two substances, a quantitative analysis was performed on real samples with recoveries of 90.70%∼104.29%. Overall, this study provided a promising technology for chemical conversion from low-cost chamomile residue to attractive bifunctional fluorescent probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an azo synthetic dye, tartrazine is popularly used in food processing to brighten the color and enhance people’s appetite, but excessive intake may cause allergies, diarrhea, and even affect the intellectual development of children (He & Du, 2023; Yuan et al., 2023; Zoughi et al., 2021). Trivalent iron ions (Fe(III)) is one of the essential trace elements in the human body, which attends various physiological processes of human metabolism (Gu et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023). Excessive or insufficient Fe(III) may induce various diseases, such as iron deficiency may lead to anemia and decreased immunity, etc., while excessive iron may lead to imbalance of the body’s oxidative system, and could induce multi-organ tumors in severe cases (Gu et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023). Therefore, both tartrazine and Fe(III) detection is important for food safety.

Various methods can be used for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection. For example, UV–vis, near-infrared (NIR), Raman spectroscopy, and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) can be used for tartrazine detection (He & Du, 2023; Yuan et al., 2023). While atomic emission spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry can be used for Fe(III) detection (Zhou et al., 2023). All these methods can detect the target substances accurately, but they usually require stringent operation procedures with special instruments, and the detections are relatively time-consuming with low selectivity (Refat et al., 2021; Singh et al., 2022). In addition to these methods, fluorometry is widely used for the detection of many substances because of its easy operation and high sensitivity, and it is also suitable for tartrazine or Fe(III) detection (He & Du, 2023; Yuan et al., 2023). However, most studies related to tartrazine detection or Fe(III) fluorescence detection merely focus on the detection of one material and the fluorescent probes used for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection are usually different (Zhou et al., 2023; Zoughi et al., 2021). In this situation, it is wise and necessary to develop a bifunctional fluorescent probe for both tartrazine and Fe(III) detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, have advantages of high stability, adjustable emission, low or no toxicity, and good biocompatibility, which make them attractive for application as fluorescent probes in bio/chemical sensing, photocatalytic and optoelectronic components, medical diagnostics, etc. (Kaur et al., 2023; Zhang & Yu, 2016). Currently, both food additives (e.g., tartrazine, myricetin, vitamin B, etc.) and metal ions (e.g., Fe(III), Cu(II), Hg(II), etc.) have been detected by CDs in many studies (Kaur et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021c; Yuan et al., 2023). However, the CDs previously used for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection are generally different, and the sensing function of CDs only limits to the detection of one kind of substance (Kaur et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021b). As a result, CDs have great potential as fluorescent probes in tartrazine and Fe(III) detection.

In recent decades, finding suitable feedstocks and exploring advanced methods were two hot research topics in CDs synthesis (Guo et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021b). Both special compounds (e.g., citric acid, ethylenediamine, dopamine, etc.) and natural feedstock (e.g., plant biomass, micro-organisms, lignocellulosic waste, etc.) could be used for CDs production (Bressi et al., 2023). Among various natural feedstocks, plant biomass such as various flowers and leaves residue, discarded shells, waste wood, etc., is an attractive substrate for CDs production for its advantages such as low-cost, great availability, and renewable characteristics (Bressi et al., 2023; Kaur et al., 2023). Currently, many methods including the “top-down” method (e.g., laser ablation, chemical oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, etc.) and “bottom-up” (e.g., direct pyrolysis, microwave, hydrothermal/solvothermal strategy, etc.) could be used for CDs synthesis (Li et al., 2021b; Zhang et al., 2022b). Among these methods, hydrothermal/solvothermal method is widely used for biomass derived CDs synthesis for its advantages such as simple, homogeneous, stable, environmentally friendly, and high efficiency (Gu et al., 2021; Passos Zattar et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a).

For plant biomass conversion especially in biorefinery, pretreatment of biomass is usually necessary to improve the efficiency of conversion (Bressi et al., 2023). Typically, chemical, physical, and biological methods can be used for biomass pretreatment (Mankar et al., 2021; Zhou & Tian, 2022). Among these methods, chemical method mainly using various solvents is widely used for biomass pretreatment for its advantages such as selectively separating the components (e.g., cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, etc.) in plant biomass, ease of operation, and relatively low-cost (Mankar et al., 2021; Ullah et al., 2023). Rather than traditional chemical pretreatment usually using organic solvents, using novel green solvents such as ionic liquids (ILs) and deep eutectic solvents (DESs) for biomass pretreatment is attractive in biorefinery to make the biomass conversion greener and cleaner (Bressi et al., 2023; Mankar et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Compared with ILs, DESs have more advantages, such as easy synthesis, low volatility, non-flammability, biodegradability, and low toxicity (Ullah et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021). Obviously, DESs have great potential for biomass pretreatment and thus promote the chemical conversion from biomass to CDs (Ullah et al., 2023). Moreover, adding N, S, Cl, and other elements selectively can modify the surface of CDs, and thus effectively enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs (Bressi et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022b). Considering these points, using DESs containing multiple elements (N, S, Cl, etc.) for biomass pretreatment and followed by a one-pot solvothermal reaction can produce CDs with good performance.

Chamomile (German chamomile) is a popular medicinal plant and a variety of chamomile-related products can be found around the world (Raal et al., 2012; Rathore & Kumar, 2021). The substances exerting pharmacological activity in chamomile are mainly flavonoids, polyphenols and essential oil, which can be separated from the dried flowers by distillation, and the chamomile residue after distillation is considered useless and discarded (Rathore & Kumar, 2021). Usually, the extraction rate of essential oils is usually only 0.10–0.61% by distillation (Raal et al., 2012), and therefore a large amount of chamomile residue will be generated in this process. Like the biorefinery of other biomass waste which can fulfill both waste management and resource utilization (Bressi et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2022), exploration of high value-added conversion of chamomile residue is a wise strategy to solve its environmental issue. Although some plant biomass residues have been utilized as feedstock for CDs synthesis (Bressi et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2022), few studies have used chamomile residue for CDs production.

In this study, a ternary DES was used as solvent for chamomile residue pretreatment and following solvothermal CDs synthesis for the first time. The effect of DES pretreatment on the CDs performance was learned to build a suitable system for CDs synthesis. The structure and optical performance of CDs were characterized, and the possibility and potential of using chamomile residue derived CDs for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection was evaluated. Overall, this study can provide some useful information for application of chamomile residue derived CDs in tartrazine and Fe(III) detection.

Chamomile (German chamomile) is purchased from Jinhua Cailuren Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Jinhua, China). The dried chamomile residue is screened through an 80-mesh filter and stored. Choline chloride (CC), DL-malic acid, sodium benzoate, D-xylose, sodium citrate, BaCl2·2H2O, NiCl2·6H2O and CoCl2 were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea (U), lactic acid (LA), quinine sulfate (biological reagent), sucrose, citric acid, l-ascorbic acid, KCl, NaCl, CuSO4·5H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, and MnSO4·H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tartrazine (TZ), sunset yellow FCF (SY), carmine (CAR), amaranth (AM), rhodamine B, aspartame, sorbic acid, citric acid, l-histidine, taurine, vitamin B6 hydrochloride, nicotinic acid, potassium sorbate, sodium nitrite, Al3(NO3)3·9H2O and CdCl2 were purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). D-Glucose, CaCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, LiCl and NH4Cl were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjing, China). MgSO4·7H2O was purchased from Wuxi Yatai United Chemical Co., Ltd, China. D-fructose was purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). L-alanine was purchased from Shanghai Boao Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). L-lysine was purchased from Yuanju Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All unspecified chemicals are analytical grade reagents and used without further purification.

CDs were synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method. Briefly, choline chloride, lactic acid and urea were weighed in a certain molar ratio (1:2:2) and then mixed and stirred at 80 °C for 30 min to obtain a colorless and transparent DES solution. The dried chamomile residue was mixed with the above DES solution with a solid–liquid ratio of 1:15, and stirred at 80 °C for 2 h, then transferred to a 25 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the dark brown reaction material was washed out with an appropriate amount of deionized water and sonicated for 5 min to make the mixture containing CDs well dispersed. Then, the crude extract containing CDs was obtained by vacuum filtration, and then dialyzed with dialysis bags (MW, 1000 DA) for 36 h, during which the water was changed every 12 h. Finally, the water was removed by a freeze dryer to obtain pure CDs powder and stored in desiccators for further use. The same procedure was repeated at various pretreatment conditions (1–4 h, 60–90 °C) and reaction conditions (4–8 h, 180–200 °C) to explore the suitable conditions for synthesis of CDs.

The fluorescence spectrum of CDs was determined by an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The UV–vis absorbance of CDs was determined by a UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The functional groups of CDs samples were determined by FT-IR (Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) in a wavenumber from 500 to 4000 cm−1. The crystal structure of CDs samples was measured by XRD (SmartLab, Rigku, Japan) with Cu radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) operated at 40 kV and 30 mA, and scanning regions from 5° to 60° (2θ) in step size of 0.02° (2θ) with scanning speed of 2°/min. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of CDs was measured by XPS (Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using Al Kα source. The morphology of CDs samples was observed by SEM (JSM-7610 F PLUS, JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were measured by a transient absorption spectroscopy (TA FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). The zeta potentials of CDs were determined by a nanoparticle size potentiometer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

The QY of the synthesized CDs were calculated using quinine sulfate (QY was 54%) dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid as a reference. The fluorescence intensity and absorbance values of quinine sulfate at 360 nm excitation wave were recorded, and the fluorescence peak areas were calculated in the wavelength range of 380–520 nm. The QY was calculated by comparing the data of CDs under the same excitation conditions according to the equation (1) (Vijeata et al., 2022):
(1)
Q c
=
Q s
(
I c
/
I s
)
×
(
A s
/
A c
)
×
(
η
c
2
/
η
s
2
)

where “Q” is the quantum yield, “I” is the fluorescence peak area, “A” is the UV–vis absorbance, and “ η ” is the solvent refractive index. The subscripts “c” and “s” represent CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively.

Typically, 1 mL of tartrazine or Fe(III) solution was added to 3 mL CDs solution to explore the sensitivity of CDs to different concentration of the samples. Among them, the concentration of CDs was 10 μg/mL, the concentration range of tartrazine was 0–187 μM, and the concentration range of Fe(III) was 0–100 μM. For the assay, fitted curves were built by monitoring the original (F0) and quenched (F) fluorescence excited by 360 nm wavelength, plotting the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) against the concentration of Fe(III) or tartrazine.

In addition, 24 kinds of food additives commonly used with tartrazine (sunset yellow, carmine, amaranth, rhodamine B, citric acid, DL-malic acid, lactic acid, sorbic acid, potassium sorbate, sodium citrate, sodium benzoate, sodium nitrite, l-ascorbic acid, nicotinic acid, vitamin B6, L-histidine, L-alanine, L-lysine, taurine, sucrose, aspartame, D-glucose, D-xylose, D-fructose) were used to verify the selectivity of CDs to tartrazine, and 14 metal ions (K+, Na+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ni+, Li+, NH4 +, Al3+, Cd2+) were used to examine the selectivity of CDs to Fe(III). The concentration of the food additive was 0.1 mM and the concentration of the metal ions was 1 mM, and the test was carried out in triplicate.

The real samples were tested by the spiked method (Vijeata et al., 2022). Lake water from the school artificial lake and the tap water from the laboratory faucet were chosen for Fe(III) detection. The beverages and sugar tablets purchased in the local supermarket were used for the detection of tartrazine. According to their ingredient list, the purchased beverages and sugar tablets contained no tartrazine, which can guarantee the validity of the spiked data. The initial concentration of Fe(III) in lake water and tap water were determined by Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 7800, Agilent Technologies, USA). Lake water and tap water were filtered with 0.45 μm filter membranes. The beverages were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and then filtered through 0.45 μm filter membranes. The sugar tablets were ground into powder, 1 g of the powder was added to 50 mL of deionized water, sonicated at 37 °C for 30 min, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and filtered with a 0.45 μm filter membrane. The above liquids were used for preparing tartrazine or Fe(III) solution with a certain concentration for detection. The test was carried out in triplicate, and the measurement results were averaged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of DES pretreatment on the synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In previous studies, the role of DESs in biomass pretreatment was mainly structural separation of lignocellulose, extracting high-value compounds from biomass, and forming small molecule compounds more suitable for value-added utilization (Ullah et al., 2023; Zhou & Tian, 2022). Recently, it was also found that DESs were suitable solvents for pretreatment and solvothermal CDs synthesis from various feedstock (tea residue, alkali lignin, molasses, etc.) for their advantages such as selectively dissolving special components and also offering special elements for CDs synthesis (Guo et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022). Currently, most DESs used for CDs synthesis are binary. Although the binary DES already has a good pretreatment effect, the addition of a third component to the binary DES to build the ternary DES may bring new properties and induce the synergistic effect of the components, which make the DES more suitable for biomass pretreatment (Ullah et al., 2023).

Generally, DESs can be divided into acidic DESs and alkaline DESs. For acidic binary DESs such as CC/LA, more precursors can be dissociated from biomass for CDs synthesis by the DES pretreatment with acidic condition (Guo et al., 2022). On the other hand, for the alkaline binary DESs such as K2CO3-glycerol, more inorganic compounds can be dissolved by the DES pretreatment with alkaline condition (Suopajärvi et al., 2020). Considering the properties of both acidic DESs and alkaline DESs, a ternary DES consisting of choline chloride (CC), lactic acid (LA), and urea (U) was designed for chamomile residue pretreatment and solvothermal CDs synthesis. To evaluate the beneficial effect of this ternary DES (CC/LA/U) on the chamomile residue derived CDs production, binary DESs including CC/LA and CC/U were also used for the same process, and the control experiment without DES (deionized water instead of DES) was also carried out.

Typically, quantum yield (QY) is a key indicator of the fluorescence properties, which is used to evaluate the performance of CDs (Kaur et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022b; Zhang & Yu, 2016). The chamomile residue derived CDs solvothermal synthesized by ternary DES (CC/LA/U) pretreatment had a QY value of 47.34% under a suitable condition (pretreatment with DES at 80 °C for 2 h, and hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 6 h). While the QY of chamomile residue derived CDs obtained with CC/LA pretreatment, CC/U pretreatment, and no DES pretreatment (control experiment) under the same condition were 19.76%, 5.56%, and 14.74%, respectively (Table 1 ). Compared with the binary DESs, the ternary DES pretreatment showed an obvious better effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs. It is possible that the weakly acidic environment of ternary DES was favorable for biomass dissolution, and thus more substrates could be used for further solvothermal CDs production (Jiang et al., 2020; Ullah et al., 2023). On the other hand, the urea component could provide nitrogen source for the synthesis of CDs to incorporate different nitrogen-containing functional groups, which facilitated CDs to exhibit higher QY (Zhang et al., 2022a; Zhang & Yu, 2016).

In addition to build a suitable solvent system for CDs production, the effect of DES pretreatment condition and solvothermal reaction condition on the fluorescence properties of chamomile residue derived CDs was also learned. As shown in Table 1, insufficient DES pretreatment time hindered the CDs production seriously, while too long time and too high temperature of DES pretreatment caused a drop in QY of CDs. At a fixed solvothermal reaction condition (200 °C for 6 h), the highest QY of CDs was obtained with DES pretreatment at 80 °C for 2 h. In addition to DES pretreatment condition, the effect of solvothermal reaction condition was further evaluated. As depicted in Table 1, the QY of CDs raised abruptly and reached a maximum when the reaction time increased to 6 h and the reaction temperature increased to 200 °C. It is worth noting that too high reaction temperature might cause DES degradation and unstable carbonization process during CDs synthesis (El Achkar et al., 2021; Kaur et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021b). Also, a long solvothermal process at high temperature will cause more energy consumption (Huang et al., 2022), thus a relatively mild solvothermal reaction condition is more proper for large-scale application. Considering above points, a suitable DES pretreatment condition (80 °C for 2 h) and solvothermal reaction condition (200 °C for 6 h) was chosen for CDs synthesis and the CDs with good fluorescence properties (QY = 47.34%) were obtained stably under this condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization of chamomile residue derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: After the pretreatment and solvothermal conversion by the ternary DES (CC/LA/U), the chamomile residue derived CDs with high QY were obtained. And then, the structure of the CDs was learned systematically by a series of characterization including scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

Firstly, the micromorphology of the prepared CDs was observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, most of the CDs exhibited a round or elliptical shape, which was also observed in some previous studies (Zoughi et al., 2021). It could also be seen in the SEM image that there were some agglomerations of CDs, which might be related to the rich hydrophilic groups existing in the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, the particle size distribution of CDs ranged from 4.49 to 14.23 nm, with an average particle size of 9.19 nm. Compared with many previous studies (Zhang et al., 2022a), the particle size of CDs synthesized in this study was relatively bigger, which was probably due to the addition of urea in the ternary DES, resulting in the presence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs (Li et al., 2021c; Zhang et al., 2022a).

Moreover, the phase and degree of crystallinity of CDs were characterized by XRD, and it can be seen in the XRD pattern that there were two broad diffraction peaks at 22.86° and 42.72° (Fig. 1C), corresponding to graphite lattice spacing (002) and (100), respectively, indicating that the synthesized CDs had an amorphous graphite structure (Atchudan et al., 2017; Liang et al., 2021).The d-spacing value of 0.39 nm was calculated using the Bragg equation (2) based on the lattice spacing (002), suggesting that the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups in the CDs introduced during the synthesis might be loaded, making the d-spacing values at this position larger than that of the bulk graphite (0.34–0.35 nm), which was similar to the structure of the phase of CDs synthesized in the previous study (Atchudan et al., 2017).
(2)
n λ = 2 d s i n θ
o r
d =
n λ
2 s i n θ
where “n” is a positive integer (1), “λ” is the wavelength of the incident X-ray (λ = 1.54 Å) and “θ” is the angle between the incident rays and the surface of the materials.

Additionally, the surface functional group distribution of CDs was measured by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1D, the broad peak at 3280.857 cm−1 and the peak at 2931.799 cm−1 were attributed to the OH and CH/NH stretching vibrations (Liang et al., 2021; Refat et al., 2021), and the peaks at 1591.493 cm−1 and 1043.318 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and COC stretching vibrations, respectively (Han et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2021). Besides, the CDs had characteristic peaks corresponding to nitrogen doping at 1648.384 cm−1 (CN), 1381.287 cm−1 (CN), and 1126.725 cm−1 (C-NH-C) (Han et al., 2016; Li et al., 2021a), indicating that the synthesized CDs contained nitrogen functional groups.

Finally, the surface elemental composition of CDs was analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1E, three peaks at 284.65 eV, 399.76 eV, and 532.16 eV belonged to C1s, N1s and O1s (Li et al., 2021a; Wu et al., 2022), and the percentages of C, N and O elements were 69.13%, 10.8% and 20.07%, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of these three elements were further measured (Fig. 1F-H) (Atchudan et al., 2017; Vijeata et al., 2022). The spectrum of C1s can be decomposed into three peaks associated with CC/CC (284.65 eV), COC (286.02 eV), and N-CO (287.96 eV), respectively (Fig. 1F) (Khan et al., 2022; Qi et al., 2022). The spectrum of O1s can be mainly divided into two peaks of N-CO (530.88 eV) and C-OH/COC (532.16 eV) (Fig. 1G) (Atchudan et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2022). The peaks of N1s can be attributed to NH (399.94 eV) and CN (399.27 eV), respectively (Fig. 1H) (Nandi et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2022). Overall, the XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results, again confirming that the CDs had been prepared successfully by the solvothermal conversion. Apparently, the chamomile residue derived CDs synthesized in this study were rich in hydrophilic functional groups such as –COOH, –CONH-, CN, and –NH-, which made them more soluble and stable in water (Khan et al., 2022), and the lone pair of electrons on O and N atoms might interact with large π-bonds to emit intense fluorescence (Qi et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of chamomile residue derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As the CDs obtained in this study had a high QY, their optical properties were learned systematically to evaluate their potential in fluorescence detection.

Firstly, UV–vis spectrophotometry and excitation/emission spectra of the CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the peaks of the excitation spectrum and the emission spectrum appeared at 358 nm and 438 nm, respectively. The UV–vis spectrum showed a distinct absorption band in the range of 200–260 nm, which might be caused by the π-π* electronic transition of the conjugated system containing the aromatic π system (Singh et al., 2022; Wu & Lee, 2020). Furthermore, as shown in the inset image in Fig. 2A, the CDs solution showed a clear blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (right) while it was transparent under incandescent (left). The fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous solution were further determined at the excitation waves from 300 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 2B), and it could be seen that the maximum emission wavelength showed different degrees of red shift with increasing excitation wavelength, suggesting that the CDs had several size variation and emissive trap states (Nandi et al., 2021). Considering the above results comprehensively, the fluorescence spectrum was more stable with 360 nm excitation wavelength, and this wavelength was applied in the following studies.

Fluorescence stability is important for ensuring the accuracy and reliability of the detection by CDs, and therefore the effect of time, pH value, ion concentration, and temperature on the fluorescence of CDs was further learned. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs fluctuated slightly under the continuous irradiation of 360 nm excitation wave, suggesting that the CDs retained a relatively good fluorescence stability within 15 days. Fig. 2D shows the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs in the solutions with pH from 2 to 12. As it depicted, there was a clear decrease in fluorescence intensity in CDs solutions with strong acidic or alkaline pH value, but the fluorescence intensity was stable within the relatively wide pH range between 4 and 9. Meanwhile, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was investigated in the CDs solution with different NaCl concentrations. As shown in Fig. 2E, with the increase of Na+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs merely showed a slight irregular fluctuation, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of CDs still had a good stability under high salt concentration. Fig. 2F shows the variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs at different temperatures (10–80 °C). As it depicted, no obvious change of CDs fluorescence intensity was found at this temperature range, showing that the CDs had a relatively good fluorescence temperature stability. Considering above results, the CDs synthesized in this study had good acid/alkali tolerance and ions resistance, and time and temperature stability, which can ensure accurate and reliable results in routine detection (Liang et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by tartrazine and Fe(III)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the capability of CDs in both food additives and metal ions detection, the fluorescence responses of CDs to tartrazine and Fe(III) were investigated systematically in this part.

As food additives have been widely applied nowadays, food additives detection to ensure their concentrations within the safe range is important (Kaur et al., 2023). Usually, the food containing tartrazine also has a variety of other food additives, therefore, the selectivity of CDs on different food additives was evaluated firstly. As shown in Fig. 3 A, most food additives did not cause obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs except for several synthetic colorants (tartrazine, sunset yellow, carmine and amaranth), revealing that these azo colorants were more likely to induce different degrees of fluorescence quenching of CDs. In particular, the highest fluorescence quenching of CDs was observed in the solution with tartrazine adding, suggesting the CDs synthesized in this study had a good selectivity for tartrazine detection. Moreover, among the azo colorants, only the CDs solution with tartrazine adding showed a red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength, and the maximum emission wavelengths with other colorants adding were all around 430 nm (Fig. 3B), suggesting that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching caused by tartrazine was different.

As the chamomile residue derived CDs had a good selectivity for tartrazine detection, the relationship between the tartrazine concentration and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was further measured. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence of CDs solution quenched gradually with the higher tartrazine concentration. When the tartrazine concentration was lower than 93.6 μM, the maximum emission wavelength was around 430 nm. With higher tartrazine concentration, the maximum emission wavelength was red-shifted slightly, and the maximum emission wavelength was 482 nm when the tartrazine concentration was 187.1 μM. Fig. 3D shows that there was a good linear relationship between the tartrazine concentration (C(TZ)) and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F). The fitted linear regression equation is F0/F = 0.01673C(TZ) + 0.9959, with the determination coefficient (R 2) of 0.9957 and the limit of detection (LOD) of 40 nM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) within the tartrazine concentration range of 0–56.1 μM.

Besides food additives, the bifunctional CDs were also used for metal ions detection. As shown in Fig. 3E, among the 15 metal ions detected, the fluorescence intensity of CDs had a significant quenching with Fe(III) adding, indicating that the CDs had an excellent selectivity for Fe(III). Moreover, the Fe(III) concentration (CFe(III)) and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) had a good linear relationship within the Fe(III) concentration range of 0–100 μM (Fig. 3F), where the fitted linear regression equation is F0/F = 0.01165CFe(III)) + 0.9896, with the R2 of 0.9943 and the LOD of 119 nM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3).

Reproducibility is an important index to evaluate the potential of actual application of CDs in fluorescence detection. To investigate the reproducibility of the detection method built in this study, five groups of CDs prepared from different batches were used for detecting tartrazine (0.1 mM) and Fe(III) (1 mM). As shown in Fig. 3G, the F0/F values of the five parallel samples for detecting tartrazine or Fe(III) were stable, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 7.35% and 6.46%, respectively, indicating that this method is reproducible in actual application (Liu et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021a).

In previous studies of CDs probes, it was common to use CDs for detecting heavy metal ions, hazardous substances, etc. in the samples, or by taking advantage of the redox reaction to achieve bifunctional detection (Gu et al., 2021; Han et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2022). For example, Fe(III) could quench the fluorescence of CDs, while the addition of l-ascorbic acid could reduce Fe(III) to Fe(II) and restore fluorescence, and through this fluorescence “off–on” mechanism to fulfill the detection of both substances (Gu et al., 2021). The bifunctional CDs synthesized in this study can not only detect both tartrazine and Fe(III), but also have the advantages of simple operation and high stability, therefore it can expand the application of CDs as the multifunctional fluorescent probes.

The luminescence and fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs have been extensively studied in the recent years, among which the common fluorescence quenching mechanisms are mainly static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filtering effect (IFE) and photoelectron transfer (PET) (Kaur et al., 2023; Mondal et al., 2023; Zu et al., 2017). As the chamomile residue derived CDs synthesized in this study were a bifunctional fluorescent probe, it is necessary to investigate the potential mechanism of fluorescence quenching caused by tartrazine and Fe(III) to further elucidate this fluorescence phenomenon.

To learn the difference between the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by tartrazine and other colorants, the change of fluorescence lifetime (take tartrazine (TZ) and sunset yellow (SY) as examples), fluorescence intensity at different temperatures, and UV-absorption spectra were investigated. The fluorescence lifetimes of the mixtures of CDs, CDs with sunset yellow (CDs-SY), CDs with tartrazine (CDs-TZ) are showed in Fig. 4 A. As it depicted, the increase in decay time caused by the addition of tartrazine to CDs suggests that the mechanism of CDs fluorescence quenching by tartrazine was possibly dynamic quenching rather than FRET or PET (Vijeata et al., 2022; Zu et al., 2017). In contrast, when the same concentration of sunset yellow was added to the CDs solution, the decay time did not change much (the ratio was about 1), suggesting that the fluorescence diminution may be caused by static quenching or IFE (Zhang et al., 2022b; Zu et al., 2017).

Typically, the ground-state complex formed by static quenching is more easily dispersed with the increase of temperature, resulting in a trend of higher fluorescence intensity; while dynamic quenching is the process with energy loss due to the collision of fluorescent substance by the quencher, and the increase of temperature will activate this process and then intensify the fluorescence quenching (Wang et al., 2021; Zu et al., 2017). Based on this, the possible mechanism of CDs fluorescence quenching caused by tartrazine and sunset yellow was further verified by the variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 4B, it is worth noting that the degree of fluorescence quenching in the tartrazine group increased and then decreased with the higher temperature (5 °C up to room temperature, and then up to 70 °C), possibly caused by the combined effect of dynamic quenching and static quenching, and this multiple fluorescence quenching mechanism has also been mentioned in previous studies (Mondal et al., 2023). In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of colorants with and without CDs were measured (Fig. 4C). In the wavelength range from 200 nm to 400 nm, the absorption curves of solution containing the colorants with CDs was slightly higher than those without CDs, though the absorption curves were very similar between these two groups. Only the tartrazine group had no obvious absorption peak in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm, where might overlap the emission spectra of CDs (Fig. 2A), suggesting that IFE could be one of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs by colorants such as sunset yellow (Nandi et al., 2021). Based on the above discussion, it can be speculated that the fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by tartrazine might be the result of a combination of dynamic and static quenching, while the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of other colorants is more likely to be mediated by static quenching and IFE (Wu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a).

Currently, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs by Fe(III) to is still controversial. Some studies suggested that the fluorescence lifetime remained stable, and the new absorption peak appeared in the UV spectrum after the addition of Fe(III) to CDs, which was an indication of static quenching (Han et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2022). While some studies inferred that CDs underwent dynamic quenching with Fe(III) based on properties such as retention of the characteristic peaks in the UV spectra but change of fluorescence lifetime and zeta potential (Cai et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021c). In the present study, it is speculated that the addition of Fe(III) to CDs caused dynamic quenching because the UV absorption spectra of CDs and Fe(III) almost overlapped (Fig. 4D) (Li et al., 2021c). As shown in Fig. 4E, the zeta potential results showed that the CDs had a negative zeta potential (-12.6 mV), while the addition of Fe(III) resulted in the positive zeta potential (6.46 mV) due to its strong positive charge density and electron-absorbing property, further demonstrating the dynamic quenching between the CDs and Fe(III) probably caused by electron transfer (Cai et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tartrazine and Fe(III) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the feasibility of CDs in practical applications of detection, the concentration of tartrazine or Fe(III) in some water samples were determined. Specifically, the concentration of tartrazine and Fe(III) detected in real samples was calculated based on two linear equations (F0/F = 0.01673C(TZ) + 0.9959, F0/F = 0.01165C(Fe(III)) + 0.9896). The recoveries were obtained by comparing the spiked and detected amounts, which ranged from 90.70% to 104.29% with RSD (n = 3) less than 1% (Table 2 ), indicating that the described method is accurate and reliable for the detection in complex solution systems containing numerous potential interferents due to selective fluorescence quenching by tartrazine and Fe(III) in these real samples (Vijeata et al., 2022).

In this study, a promising chemical conversion from chamomile residue to bifunctional CDs used for tartrazine and Fe(III) detection by the pretreatment of a ternary DES has been fulfilled successfully. Overall, this method is environmentally friendly, cost-controllable, and ease for operation. Under UV irradiation, the synthesized CDs were able to exhibit bright blue fluorescence at low concentrations, with QY of 47.34% under appropriate preparation condition. Moreover, the excellent fluorescence properties and stability of the chamomile residue derived CDs make them be the suitable fluorescent probe for detection of tartrazine and Fe(III), which can cause significant fluorescence quenching of CDs within a short time after mixing. The tartrazine and Fe(III) detection in actual samples confirmed that the CDs have great potential for application in the field of food safety.

Zheng Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Han-Yi Zheng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhong-Ying Huang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yue-Zhen Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yao-Hua Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yun Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jing Gao: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yong Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chao Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Special project in key fields of colleges and universities in Guangdong Province (2021ZDZX4001), the Science and Technology Program of Zhongshan (2021B2001, 2020B2068, 2021B2048), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019A1515011843, 2019A1515010640, 2021A1515110999), Special Support Project of Guangdong Province (2016TQ03N881), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078070).
